Abstract: We introduce `Goxpyriment', a new open-source software framework for programming behavioral and cognitive experiments using the Go programming language. The library is designed to address some limitations of existing Python-based experiment tools, particularly the runtime environment complexity that frequently complicates deployment across laboratories. Because Go is a compiled language that can natively embed assets (e.g., graphics, audio files, and stimulus lists), Goxpyriment compiles entire experiments into single, self-contained executable binaries with zero runtime dependencies. This drastically simplifies distribution to collaborators and testing computers. The programming interface, inspired by Expyriment (Krause & Lindemann, 2014), was designed to be human friendly. The library includes an array of visual stimuli (text, shapes, images, Gabor patches, motion clouds, ...) and audio capabilities (WAV playback and tone generation). While developing Goxpyriment, we focused on timing reliability. Input events are timestamped by the operating system at hardware-interrupt time, so reaction times are computed by subtracting two OS-level timestamps rather than relying on continuous polling. Go's garbage collector can be disabled, greatly reducing the probability of unpredictable pauses that could corrupt stimulus timing. Finally, a set of over forty psychology experiments implemented in Goxpyriment are provided that promote not only learning by humans but also improve the ability of modern AI-assisted coding tools to help program experiments. The framework is released under the
  GNU General Public License v3 and is freely available at https://github.com/chrplr/goxpyriment.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [421] [Doubly Outlier-Robust Online Infinite Hidden Markov Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14322)
*Horace Yiu,Leandro Sánchez-Betancourt,Álvaro Cartea,Gerardo Duran-Martin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Batched Robust infinite Hidden Markov Model (BR-iHMM), a method to enhance online sequential modeling with robustness in presence of outliers and model misspecification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in online learning impacted by outliers and model misspecification, ensuring better forecasting and interpretability in various domains.

Method: The authors introduce BR-iHMM, leveraging generalized Bayesian inference with the Posterior Influence Function (PIF), incorporating tunable parameters to balance adaptivity and robustness.

Result: The method demonstrated up to a 67% improvement in forecasting accuracy in real-world and synthetic datasets, supported by theoretical guarantees of robustness.

Conclusion: BR-iHMM effectively manages adaptivity and robustness, making it suitable for accurate and interpretable online learning tasks in diverse applications.

Abstract: We derive a robust update rule for the online infinite hidden Markov model (iHMM) for when the streaming data contains outliers and the model is misspecified. Leveraging recent advances in generalised Bayesian inference, we define robustness via the posterior influence function (PIF), and provide conditions under which the online iHMM has bounded PIF. Imposing robustness inevitably induces an adaptation lag for regime switching. Our method, which is called Batched Robust iHMM (BR-iHMM), balances adaptivity and robustness with two additional tunable parameters. Across limit order book data, hourly electricity demand, and a synthetic high-dimensional linear system, BR-iHMM reduces one-step-ahead forecasting error by up to 67% relative to competing online Bayesian methods. Together with theoretical guarantees of bounded PIF, our results highlight the practicality of our approach for both forecasting and interpretable online learning.

</details>


### [422] [Differentially Private Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14621)
*Jiamei Wu,Ce Zhang,Zhipeng Cai,Jingsen Kong,Bei Jiang,Linglong Kong,Lingchen Kong*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces Differentially Private Conformal Prediction (DPCP), which combines differential privacy mechanisms with conformal prediction for efficient and accurate uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to adapt conformal prediction methods to work efficiently under the constraints of differential privacy, maintaining both statistical validity and privacy guarantees.

Method: The paper introduces a non-splitting differential CP framework to directly link private conformal methods to oracle CP. It further extends the framework into DPCP, which integrates private model training and quantile mechanisms for calibration.

Result: DPCP demonstrates tighter prediction sets and superior efficiency compared to existing private split conformal methods under the same privacy budget, validated through theoretical analysis and numerical experiments.

Conclusion: The proposed DPCP approach effectively balances statistical efficiency and privacy, providing practical and reliable uncertainty quantifications for predictive models.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) has attracted broad attention as a simple and flexible framework for uncertainty quantification through prediction sets. In this work, we study how to deploy CP under differential privacy (DP) in a statistically efficient manner. We first introduce differential CP, a non-splitting conformal procedure that avoids the efficiency loss caused by data splitting and serves as a bridge between oracle CP and private conformal inference. By exploiting the stability properties of DP mechanisms, differential CP establishes a direct connection to oracle CP and inherits corresponding validity behavior. Building on this idea, we develop Differentially Private Conformal Prediction (DPCP), a fully private procedure that combines DP model training with a private quantile mechanism for calibration. We establish the end-to-end privacy guarantee of DPCP and investigate its coverage properties under additional regularity conditions. We further study the efficiency of both differential CP and DPCP under empirical risk minimization and general regression models, showing that DPCP can produce tighter prediction sets than existing private split conformal approaches under the same privacy budget. Numerical experiments on synthetic and real datasets demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods.

</details>


### [423] [Expert-Guided Class-Conditional Goodness-of-Fit Scores for Interpretable Classification with Informative Missingness: An Application to Seismic Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14809)
*Shahar Cohen,David M. Steinberg,Yael Radzyner,Yochai Ben Horin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework designed to address classification challenges involving missing data, expert knowledge integration, and interpretability. It applies the method to seismic monitoring for nuclear-test-ban treaty compliance, showcasing strong potential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses three pivotal challenges in classification processes: managing missing data (with informative aspects), incorporating partial expert knowledge, and creating interpretable decision rules.

Method: The framework encodes expert knowledge into a class-conditional model, derives interpretable goodness-of-fit features, combines them with auxiliary summaries, and uses a simple discriminative classifier.

Result: The proposed method demonstrated potential for seismic monitoring and outperformed standard machine-learning classifiers in simulations involving limited training samples.

Conclusion: The expert-guided framework provides interpretable, effective classification while reducing analyst workload, showing promise as a valuable screening tool.

Abstract: We study a classification problem with three key challenges: pervasive informative missingness, the integration of partial prior expert knowledge into the learning process, and the need for interpretable decision rules. We propose a framework that encodes prior knowledge through an expert-guided class-conditional model for one or more classes, and use this model to construct a small set of interpretable goodness-of-fit features. The features quantify how well the observed data agree with the expert model, isolating the contributions of different aspects of the data, including both observed and missing components. These features are combined with a few transparent auxiliary summaries in a simple discriminative classifier, resulting in a decision rule that is easy to inspect and justify. We develop and apply the framework in the context of seismic monitoring used to assess compliance with the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty. We show that the method has strong potential as a transparent screening tool, reducing workload for expert analysts. A simulation designed to isolate the contribution of the proposed framework shows that this interpretable expert-guided method can even outperform strong standard machine-learning classifiers, particularly when training samples are small.

</details>


### [424] [Scalable Model-Based Clustering with Sequential Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14810)
*Connie Trojan,Pavel Myshkov,Paul Fearnhead,James Hensman,Tom Minka,Christopher Nemeth*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) algorithm to address large-scale, uncertain online clustering problems, especially for text data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SMC methods for online clustering struggle with complexity and memory issues, especially when dealing with large-scale text data and complex cluster distributions.

Method: The authors propose decomposing clustering problems into approximately independent subproblems, enabling a more compact and efficient representation within an SMC framework.

Result: Empirical results indicate that the proposed algorithm solves knowledge base construction tasks and similar clustering problems with improved accuracy and efficiency compared to traditional SMC methods.

Conclusion: The proposed method offers a significant advancement in handling uncertain online clustering tasks for text data by reducing computational requirements and maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: In online clustering problems, there is often a large amount of uncertainty over possible cluster assignments that cannot be resolved until more data are observed. This difficulty is compounded when clusters follow complex distributions, as is the case with text data. Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) methods give a natural way of representing and updating this uncertainty over time, but have prohibitive memory requirements for large-scale problems. We propose a novel SMC algorithm that decomposes clustering problems into approximately independent subproblems, allowing a more compact representation of the algorithm state. Our approach is motivated by the knowledge base construction problem, and we show that our method is able to accurately and efficiently solve clustering problems in this setting and others where traditional SMC struggles.

</details>


### [425] [Best of both worlds: Stochastic & adversarial best-arm identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14860)
*Yasin Abbasi-Yadkori,Peter L. Bartlett,Victor Gabillon,Alan Malek,Michal Valko*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper addresses the challenge of designing a learner for a bandit best-arm identification problem that works optimally under both stochastic and adversarial reward settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from creating a single learner that can operate effectively without needing prior knowledge of whether the rewards are stochastic or adversarial, thereby providing robustness across varying reward environments.

Method: The authors provide theoretical analysis to show the impossibility of general optimality in both reward settings and derive a lower bound on error for stochastic problems under adversarial constraints. They then propose a parameter-free algorithm to approach this bound.

Result: The proposed algorithm achieves near-optimal error rates (up to log factors) in stochastic cases and maintains robustness against adversarial rewards.

Conclusion: It is impossible to design a universally optimal learner for adversarial and stochastic rewards, but the proposed algorithm demonstrates a strong trade-off performance, balancing between both settings effectively.

Abstract: We study bandit best-arm identification with arbitrary and potentially adversarial rewards. A simple random uniform learner obtains the optimal rate of error in the adversarial scenario. However, this type of strategy is suboptimal when the rewards are sampled stochastically. Therefore, we ask: Can we design a learner that performs optimally in both the stochastic and adversarial problems while not being aware of the nature of the rewards? First, we show that designing such a learner is impossible in general. In particular, to be robust to adversarial rewards, we can only guarantee optimal rates of error on a subset of the stochastic problems. We give a lower bound that characterizes the optimal rate in stochastic problems if the strategy is constrained to be robust to adversarial rewards. Finally, we design a simple parameter-free algorithm and show that its probability of error matches (up to log factors) the lower bound in stochastic problems, and it is also robust to adversarial ones.

</details>


### [426] [Unsupervised feature selection using Bayesian Tucker decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14949)
*Y-h. Taguchi,Yoh-ichi Mototake*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper presents Bayesian Tucker decomposition (BTuD), successfully applied for unsupervised feature selection across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve unsupervised feature selection by proposing a novel Bayesian Tucker decomposition (BTuD) method.

Method: BTuD is proposed with a Gaussian residual assumption, ensuring compatibility with conventional Tucker decomposition implementations.

Result: BTuD showed success in unsupervised feature selection across synthetic datasets, complex global systems, and gene expression profiles.

Conclusion: BTuD is a promising approach for unsupervised feature selection and aligns with prior tensor decomposition-based feature extraction methods.

Abstract: In this paper, we proposed Bayesian Tucker decomposition (BTuD) in which residual is supposed to obey Gaussian distribution analogous to linear regression. Although we have proposed an algorithm to perform the proposed BTuD, the conventional higher-order orthogonal iteration can generate Tucker decomposition consistent with the present implementation. Using the proposed BTuD, we can perform unsupervised feature selection successfully applied to various synthetic datasets, global coupled maps with randomized coupling strength, and gene expression profiles. Thus we can conclude that our newly proposed unsupervised feature selection method is promising. In addition to this, BTuD based unsupervised FE is expected to coincide with TD based unsupervised FE that were previously proposed and successfully applied to a wide range of problems.

</details>


### [427] [MinShap: A Modified Shapley Value Approach for Feature Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15107)
*Chenghui Zheng,Garvesh Raskutti*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces MinShap, a modification of Shapley values for better feature selection in machine learning, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature selection remains a challenging problem due to unknown non-linear relationships and dependent features, prompting the need for improved techniques.

Method: MinShap, a variation of Shapley values, utilizes the minimum marginal contribution across permutations, supported by theoretical guarantees and algorithms for lower-sample settings.

Result: MinShap demonstrates superior performance compared to LOCO, GCM, and Lasso in accuracy and stability, validated through simulations and real experiments.

Conclusion: MinShap is an effective feature selection approach that adapts Shapley values, offering advancements over existing methods especially in complex nonlinear and dependent feature contexts.

Abstract: Feature selection is a classical problem in statistics and machine learning, and it continues to remain an extremely challenging problem especially in the context of unknown non-linear relationships with dependent features. On the other hand, Shapley values are a classic solution concept from cooperative game theory that is widely used for feature attribution in general non-linear models with highly-dependent features. However, Shapley values are not naturally suited for feature selection since they tend to capture both direct effects from each feature to the response and indirect effects through other features. In this paper, we combine the advantages of Shapley values and adapt them to feature selection by proposing \emph{MinShap}, a modification of the Shapley value framework along with a suite of other related algorithms. In particular for MinShap, instead of taking the average marginal contributions over permutations of features, considers the minimum marginal contribution across permutations. We provide a theoretical foundation motivated by the faithfulness assumption in DAG (directed acyclic graphical models), a guarantee for the Type I error of MinShap, and show through numerical simulations and real data experiments that MinShap tends to outperform state-of-the-art feature selection algorithms such as LOCO, GCM and Lasso in terms of both accuracy and stability. We also introduce a suite of algorithms related to MinShap by using the multiple testing/p-value perspective that improves performance in lower-sample settings and provide supporting theoretical guarantees.

</details>


### [428] [Amortized Optimal Transport from Sliced Potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15114)
*Minh-Phuc Truong,Khai Nguyen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces two strategies, RA-OT and OA-OT, for amortized optimization of optimal transport (OT) plans using Kantorovich potentials derived from sliced OT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently solving repeated optimal transport problems across different pairs of measures, leveraging learned information to approximate solutions rapidly.

Method: RA-OT employs functional regression on Kantorovich potentials using least squares, while OA-OT optimizes a dual objective of Kantorovich potentials.

Result: Both methods achieve efficiency and high accuracy for various transport tasks, including MNIST digit transport and mini-batch OT flow matching, without dependence on specific structural constraints.

Conclusion: RA-OT and OA-OT offer parsimonious and effective solutions for amortized OT across diverse applications, reusing prior knowledge to efficiently approximate OT plans.

Abstract: We propose a novel amortized optimization method for predicting optimal transport (OT) plans across multiple pairs of measures by leveraging Kantorovich potentials derived from sliced OT. We introduce two amortization strategies: regression-based amortization (RA-OT) and objective-based amortization (OA-OT). In RA-OT, we formulate a functional regression model that treats Kantorovich potentials from the original OT problem as responses and those obtained from sliced OT as predictors, and estimate these models via least-squares methods. In OA-OT, we estimate the parameters of the functional model by optimizing the Kantorovich dual objective. In both approaches, the predicted OT plan is subsequently recovered from the estimated potentials. As amortized OT methods, both RA-OT and OA-OT enable efficient solutions to repeated OT problems across different measure pairs by reusing information learned from prior instances to rapidly approximate new solutions. Moreover, by exploiting the structure provided by sliced OT, the proposed models are more parsimonious, independent of specific structures of the measures, such as the number of atoms in the discrete case, while achieving high accuracy. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approaches on tasks including MNIST digit transport, color transfer, supply-demand transportation on spherical data, and mini-batch OT conditional flow matching.

</details>


### [429] [Structural interpretability in SVMs with truncated orthogonal polynomial kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15285)
*Víctor Soto-Larrosa,Nuria Torrado,Edmundo J. Huertas*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a post-training diagnostic framework for SVMs using truncated orthogonal polynomial kernels, called Orthogonal Representation Contribution Analysis (ORCA), leveraging normalized Orthogonal Kernel Contribution indices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve interpretability of SVMs by analyzing their internal structure post-training, without relying on surrogate models or retraining.

Method: The authors utilize intrinsic RKHS coordinates and normalized OKC indices to quantify contributions across interaction orders, polynomial degrees, and variable effects within the kernel space.

Result: ORCA reveals insights into model complexity that predictive accuracy fails to capture, demonstrated on synthetic and real datasets.

Conclusion: The methodology allows for rigorous interpretability of SVMs, enhancing understanding of structural aspects without compromising training efficiency.

Abstract: We study post-training interpretability for Support Vector Machines (SVMs) built from truncated orthogonal polynomial kernels. Since the associated reproducing kernel Hilbert space is finite-dimensional and admits an explicit tensor-product orthonormal basis, the fitted decision function can be expanded exactly in intrinsic RKHS coordinates. This leads to Orthogonal Representation Contribution Analysis (ORCA), a diagnostic framework based on normalized Orthogonal Kernel Contribution (OKC) indices. These indices quantify how the squared RKHS norm of the classifier is distributed across interaction orders, total polynomial degrees, marginal coordinate effects, and pairwise contributions. The methodology is fully post-training and requires neither surrogate models nor retraining. We illustrate its diagnostic value on a synthetic double-spiral problem and on a real five-dimensional echocardiogram dataset. The results show that the proposed indices reveal structural aspects of model complexity that are not captured by predictive accuracy alone.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [430] [Controlling Authority Retrieval: A Missing Retrieval Objective for Authority-Governed Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14488)
*Andre Bacellar*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Controlling Authority Retrieval (CAR), a method for identifying authoritative documents in domains like law, drug regulation, and software security. It formalizes the retrieval problem and proves correctness criteria, validating results across multiple real-world corpora.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address a specific retrieval challenge in domains where new knowledge voids prior documents even when semantics diverge. Properly identifying active documents from authoritative sources is critical for accuracy in such contexts.

Method: They propose a theoretical framework for CAR, establishing correctness conditions through Theorem 4 and Proposition 2, validated using adversarial permutation arguments. Several evaluations are conducted with real-world datasets and GPT-4 experiments.

Result: Proven structural correctness of CAR methodology; real-world validation across security advisories, legal cases, and FDA records. CAR reduces mistaken retrieval ('not patched' claims) significantly compared to dense RAG methods.

Conclusion: CAR offers clear benefits for authoritative document retrieval, both in theoretical guarantees and practical improvements, and contributes benchmarks and tools for future research in this domain.

Abstract: In any domain where knowledge accumulates under formal authority -- law, drug regulation, software security -- a later document can formally void an earlier one while remaining semantically distant from it. We formalize this as Controlling Authority Retrieval (CAR): recovering the active frontier front(cl(A_k(q))) of the authority closure of the semantic anchor set -- a different mathematical problem from argmax_d s(q,d). The two central results are: Theorem 4 (CAR-Correctness Characterization) gives necessary-and-sufficient conditions on any retrieved set R for TCA(R,q)=1 -- frontier inclusion and no-ignored-superseder -- independent of how R was produced. Proposition 2 (Scope Identifiability Upper Bound) establishes phi(q) as a hard worst-case ceiling: for any scope-indexed algorithm, TCA@k <= phi(q) * R_anchor(q), proved by an adversarial permutation argument. Three independent real-world corpora validate the proved structure: security advisories (Dense TCA@5=0.270, two-stage 0.975), SCOTUS overruling pairs (Dense=0.172, two-stage 0.926), FDA drug records (Dense=0.064, two-stage 0.774). A GPT-4o-mini experiment shows the downstream cost: Dense RAG produces explicit "not patched" claims for 39% of queries where a patch exists; Two-Stage cuts this to 16%. Four benchmark datasets, domain adapters, and a single-command scorer are released at https://github.com/andremir/car-retrieval.

</details>


### [431] [Don't Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills for QA and RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14572)
*Yiqun Sun,Pengfei Wei,Lawrence B. Hsieh*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Corpus2Skill, which enhances the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework by allowing LLMs to navigate a hierarchical skill directory of the document corpus, achieving better performance on a customer-support benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations of current RAG systems, where models lack awareness of corpus organization and cannot backtrack or combine dispersed evidence effectively.

Method: Corpus2Skill builds a hierarchical skill directory by clustering and summarizing documents into a tree structure offline. At serve time, an LLM navigates this hierarchy, retrieves relevant summaries, and combines evidence across branches to generate responses.

Result: Corpus2Skill outperformed existing baselines like dense retrieval, RAPTOR, and agentic RAG on the WixQA benchmark, showing superior metrics across the board.

Conclusion: Incorporating hierarchical corpus structures into RAG enables more effective reasoning, backtracking, and evidence combination. This advancement improves response quality in complex, customer-support tasks.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds LLM responses in external evidence but treats the model as a passive consumer of search results: it never sees how the corpus is organized or what it has not yet retrieved, limiting its ability to backtrack or combine scattered evidence. We present Corpus2Skill, which distills a document corpus into a hierarchical skill directory offline and lets an LLM agent navigate it at serve time. The compilation pipeline iteratively clusters documents, generates LLM-written summaries at each level, and materializes the result as a tree of navigable skill files. At serve time, the agent receives a bird's-eye view of the corpus, drills into topic branches via progressively finer summaries, and retrieves full documents by ID. Because the hierarchy is explicitly visible, the agent can reason about where to look, backtrack from unproductive paths, and combine evidence across branches. On WixQA, an enterprise customer-support benchmark for RAG, Corpus2Skill outperforms dense retrieval, RAPTOR, and agentic RAG baselines across all quality metrics.

</details>


### [432] [PriHA: A RAG-Enhanced LLM Framework for Primary Healthcare Assistant in Hong Kong](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14215)
*Richard Wai Cheung Chan,Shanru Lin,Ya-nan Ma,Hao Chen,Liangjun Jiang,Wenqi Fan*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces PriHA, a system leveraging a novel Dual Retrieval Augmented Generation architecture to overcome information accessibility issues in primary healthcare by enabling accurate and localized dialogue retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address unsustainable healthcare costs in Hong Kong by enhancing primary healthcare access and overcoming fragmented clinical guidelines barriers.

Method: The proposed system features a tri-stage pipeline: query optimizer for user intent sub-queries, Dual Retrieval Augmented Generation (DRAG) architecture for mixed-source retrieval, and context-reorganized generation.

Result: Experiments and case study indicate PriHA outperforms baselines and ablations in accuracy and clarity.

Conclusion: PriHA provides a traceable dialogue framework, potentially applicable to other high-risk, localized domains.

Abstract: To address the unsustainable rise in public health expenditures, the Hong Kong SAR Government is shifting its strategic focus to primary healthcare and encouraging citizens to use community resources to self-manage their health. However, official clinical guidelines are fragmented across disparate departments and formats, creating significant access barriers. While general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT and DeepSeek offer potential solutions for information accessibility, they are prone to generating factually inaccurate content due to a lack of localized and domain-specific knowledge. To this end, we propose a Retrieval-Augmented Generation-Enhanced LLM system as Primary Healthcare Assistant (PriHA) in Hong Kong. Specifically, a tri-stage pipeline is proposed that leverages a query optimizer to generalize user intent-oriented sub-queries, followed by a novel Dual Retrieval Augmented Generation (DRAG) architecture for mixed-source retrieval and context-reorganized generation. Comprehensive experiments and a detailed case study demonstrate that our proposed method can outperform both ablations and baseline in terms of accuracy and clarity. Our research provides a reliable and traceable dialogue retrieval framework for exploring other high-risk, localized application scenarios.

</details>


### [433] [Knowledge Graph RAG: Agentic Crawling and Graph Construction in Enterprise Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14220)
*Koushik Chakraborty,Koyel Guha*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper proposes Agentic Knowledge Graphs with Recursive Crawling to improve semantic search in enterprise document ecosystems, showing a 70% accuracy improvement in regulatory queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of traditional semantic search and RAG pipelines in handling interconnected and hierarchical information in enterprise documents.

Method: Introduce Agentic Knowledge Graphs integrated with Recursive Crawling to navigate superseding logic and multi-hop references effectively.

Result: Achieved a 70% improvement in retrieval accuracy over conventional vector-based RAG systems using the CFR dataset.

Conclusion: The Knowledge Graph-enhanced approach offers a precise and exhaustive solution for complex semantic search tasks in hierarchical enterprise documents.

Abstract: This research paper addresses the limitations of semantic search in complex enterprise document ecosystems. Traditional RAG pipelines often fail to capture hierarchical and interconnected information, leading to retrieval inaccuracies. We propose Agentic Knowledge Graphs featuring Recursive Crawling as a robust solution for navigating superseding logic and multi-hop references. Our benchmark evaluation using the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) demonstrates that this Knowledge Graph-enhanced approach achieves a 70% accuracy improvement over standard vector-based RAG systems, providing exhaustive and precise answers for complex regulatory queries.

</details>


### [434] [Adaptive Query Routing: A Tier-Based Framework for Hybrid Retrieval Across Financial, Legal, and Medical Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14222)
*Afshan Hashmi*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This study evaluates three retrieval systems—Vector RAG, Tree Reasoning, and Adaptive Hybrid Retrieval (AHR)—across multiple domains, revealing Tree Reasoning's overall superiority but emphasizing the context-specific strengths of different paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the limitations of existing retrieval paradigms by systematically comparing their performance across diverse query complexities and document structures.

Method: Researchers implement three architectures (Vector RAG, Tree Reasoning, and Adaptive Hybrid Retrieval) and benchmark their effectiveness using a four-tier query complexity scale and GPT-4-powered evaluation.

Result: Tree Reasoning achieves the highest overall score, while Hybrid AHR performs best in cross-reference and multi-section queries. Vector RAG excels in multi-document synthesis but exhibits lower cross-reference recall compared to the other systems.

Conclusion: Findings highlight the importance of context-aware adaptive retrieval systems and underline Tree Reasoning's superior quality in financial domain QA. All code and data are publicly available for further research.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become the standard paradigm for grounding Large Language Model outputs in external knowledge. Lumer et al. [1] presented the first systematic evaluation comparing vector-based agentic RAG against hierarchical node-based reasoning systems for financial document QA across 1,200 SEC filings, finding vector-based systems achieved a 68% win rate. Concurrently, the PageIndex framework [2] demonstrated 98.7% accuracy on FinanceBench through purely reasoning-based retrieval. This paper extends their work by: (i) implementing and evaluating three retrieval architectures: Vector RAG, Tree Reasoning, and the proposed Adaptive Hybrid Retrieval (AHR) across financial, legal, and medical domains; (ii) introducing a four-tier query complexity benchmark; and (iii) employing GPT-4-powered LLM-as-judge evaluation. Experiments reveal that Tree Reasoning achieves the highest overall score (0.900), but no single paradigm dominates across all tiers: Vector RAG wins on multi-document synthesis (Tier 4, score 0.900), while the Hybrid AHR achieves the best performance on cross-reference (0.850) and multi-section queries (0.929). Cross-reference recall reaches 100% for tree-based and hybrid approaches versus 91.7% for vector search, quantifying a critical capability gap. Validation on FinanceBench (150 expert-annotated questions on real SEC 10-K and 10-Q filings) confirms and strengthens these findings: Tree Reasoning scores 0.938, Hybrid AHR 0.901, and Vector RAG 0.821, with the Tree--Vector quality gap widening to 11.7 percentage points on real-world documents. These findings support the development of adaptive retrieval systems that dynamically select strategies based on query complexity and document structure. All code and data are publicly available.

</details>


### [435] [TRACE: A Conversational Framework for Sustainable Tourism Recommendation with Agentic Counterfactual Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14223)
*Ashmi Banerjee,Adithi Satish,Wolfgang Wörndl,Yashar Deldjoo*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: TRACE is a tourism recommendation system leveraging multi-agent and LLM-based frameworks to promote sustainable decisions through interactive nudging and personalized user engagement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional travel recommendation systems often favor popular, overcrowded destinations and environmentally taxing choices. TRACE seeks to counter this trend by integrating sustainability into personalized decision-making.

Method: TRACE employs a modular architecture with multiple agents that identify latent sustainability preferences, curate user personas, and optimize recommendations by blending relevance and eco-consciousness. It utilizes agentic counterfactual explanations and LLM-driven queries for enhanced user experience.

Result: User studies confirm TRACE’s effectiveness in aligning recommendations with ecological considerations without sacrificing recommendation quality or interactivity.

Conclusion: TRACE advances sustainable tourism through innovative use of LLM-driven interactivity and counterfactual reasoning, with resources fully documented for broader adoption and reproducibility.

Abstract: Traditional conversational travel recommender systems primarily optimize for user relevance and convenience, often reinforcing popular, overcrowded destinations and carbon-intensive travel choices. To address this, we present TRACE (Tourism Recommendation with Agentic Counterfactual Explanations), a multi-agent, LLM-based framework that promotes sustainable tourism through interactive nudging. TRACE uses a modular orchestrator-worker architecture where specialized agents elicit latent sustainability preferences, construct structured user personas, and generate recommendations that balance relevance with environmental impact.
  A key innovation lies in its use of agentic counterfactual explanations and LLM-driven clarifying questions, which together surface greener alternatives and refine understanding of intent, fostering user reflection without coercion. User studies and semantic alignment analyses demonstrate that TRACE effectively supports sustainable decision-making while preserving recommendation quality and interactive responsiveness. TRACE is implemented on Google's Agent Development Kit, with full code, Docker setup, prompts, and a publicly available demo video to ensure reproducibility.
  A project summary, including all resources, prompts, and demo access, is available at https://ashmibanerjee.github.io/trace-chatbot.

</details>


### [436] [FRESCO: Benchmarking and Optimizing Re-rankers for Evolving Semantic Conflict in Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14227)
*Sohyun An,Hayeon Lee,Shuibenyang Yuan,Chun-cheng Jason Chen,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Vijai Mohan,Alexander Min*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces FRESCO, a benchmark to evaluate re-rankers in dynamic contexts. It highlights re-rankers' bias for older, semantically rich but outdated documents, proposing optimization methods to address this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for re-rankers fail to assess performance in dynamic contexts, crucial for real-world retrieval-augmented generation systems that must select temporally relevant evidence.

Method: FRESCO leverages recency-seeking queries coupled with historical Wikipedia revisions to test re-rankers on their ability to prioritize up-to-date evidence.

Result: Current re-rankers consistently favor older, semantically dense documents over newer, factually relevant ones. Instruction optimization was able to improve performance in dynamic contexts by up to 27%.

Conclusion: FRESCO effectively exposes shortcomings in re-rankers under evolving conditions and demonstrates that instruction optimization can mitigate biases while balancing performance across tasks.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a key approach to mitigating the temporal staleness of large language models (LLMs) by grounding responses in up-to-date evidence. Within the RAG pipeline, re-rankers play a pivotal role in selecting the most useful documents from retrieved candidates. However, existing benchmarks predominantly evaluate re-rankers in static settings and do not adequately assess performance under evolving information -- a critical gap, as real-world systems often must choose among temporally different pieces of evidence. To address this limitation, we introduce FRESCO (Factual Recency and Evolving Semantic COnflict), a benchmark for evaluating re-rankers in temporally dynamic contexts. By pairing recency-seeking queries with historical Wikipedia revisions, FRESCO tests whether re-rankers can prioritize factually recent evidence while maintaining semantic relevance. Our evaluation reveals a consistent failure mode across existing re-rankers: a strong bias toward older, semantically rich documents, even when they are factually obsolete. We further investigate an instruction optimization framework to mitigate this issue. By identifying Pareto-optimal instructions that balance Evolving and Non-Evolving Knowledge tasks, we obtain gains of up to 27% on Evolving Knowledge tasks while maintaining competitive performance on Non-Evolving Knowledge tasks.

</details>


### [437] [Evaluation of Agents under Simulated AI Marketplace Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14256)
*To Eun Kim,Alireza Salemi,Hamed Zamani,Fernando Diaz*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Modern information ecosystems need evaluation methods beyond static benchmarks, focusing on marketplace dynamics and competitive pressures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the limitations of accuracy-focused evaluation methods, which fail to capture real-world competitive dynamics in information systems.

Method: The authors propose 'Marketplace Evaluation,' a simulation-based framework that evaluates systems in competitive marketplaces through repeated interactions and evolving preferences.

Result: The framework introduces metrics like user retention and market share to evaluate post-deployment success and dynamics.

Conclusion: Marketplace Evaluation can complement traditional methods and reshape evaluation paradigms for information access systems in competitive contexts.

Abstract: Modern information access ecosystems consist of mixtures of systems, such as retrieval systems and large language models, and increasingly rely on marketplaces to mediate access to models, tools, and data, making competition between systems inherent to deployment. In such settings, outcomes are shaped not only by benchmark quality but also by competitive pressure, including user switching, routing decisions, and operational constraints. Yet evaluation is still largely conducted on static benchmarks with accuracy-focused measures that assume systems operate in isolation. This mismatch makes it difficult to predict post-deployment success and obscures competitive effects such as early-adoption advantages and market dominance. We introduce Marketplace Evaluation, a simulation-based paradigm that evaluates information access systems as participants in a competitive marketplace. By simulating repeated interactions and evolving user and agent preferences, the framework enables longitudinal evaluation and marketplace-level metrics, such as retention and market share, that complement and can extend beyond traditional accuracy-based metrics. We formalize the framework and outline a research agenda, motivated by business and economics, around marketplace simulation, metrics, optimization, and adoption in evaluation campaigns like TREC.

</details>


### [438] [Metric-agnostic Learning-to-Rank via Boosting and Rank Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15101)
*Camilo Gomez,Pengyang Wang,Yanjie Fu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel listwise Learning-to-Rank (LTR) framework that resolves non-differentiability and generalization issues in ranking tasks, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LTR methods struggle with non-differentiability in ranking metrics and poor generalization to multiple ranking metrics, limiting their efficiency and applicability.

Method: The authors propose a new differentiable ranking loss combining a smooth approximation of the ranking operator with average mean square loss, integrated with gradient-boosting machines to optimize rankings on a listwise basis.

Result: The proposed framework achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods across prominent information retrieval metrics, without compromising efficiency.

Conclusion: This novel LTR framework enhances both efficiency and generalization in ranking tasks, addressing key limitations of existing approaches and demonstrating strong experimental results.

Abstract: Learning-to-Rank (LTR) is a supervised machine learning approach that constructs models specifically designed to order a set of items or documents based on their relevance or importance to a given query or context. Despite significant success in real-world information retrieval systems, current LTR methods rely on one prefix ranking metric (e.g., such as Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) or Mean Average Precision (MAP)) for optimizing the ranking objective function. Such metric-dependent setting limits LTR methods from two perspectives: (1) non-differentiable problem: directly optimizing ranking functions over a given ranking metric is inherently non-smooth, making the training process unstable and inefficient; (2) limited ranking utility: optimizing over one single metric makes it difficult to generalize well to other ranking metrics of interest. To address the above issues, we propose a novel listwise LTR framework for efficient and generalizable ranking purpose. Specifically, we propose a new differentiable ranking loss that combines a smooth approximation to the ranking operator with the average mean square loss per query. Then, we adapt gradient-boosting machines to minimize our proposed loss with respect to each list, a novel contribution. Finally, extensive experimental results confirm that our method outperforms the current state-of-the-art in information retrieval measures with similar efficiency.

</details>


### [439] [NewsTorch: A PyTorch-based Toolkit for Learner-oriented News Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14510)
*Rongyao Wang,Veronica Liesaputra,Zhiyi Huang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces NewsTorch, a PyTorch-based toolkit for news recommendation systems, aimed at assisting learners with conceptual understanding and hands-on experience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The lack of a specialized toolkit for learner-oriented news recommendation research discourages progress and educational engagement in the field.

Method: The paper develops NewsTorch, a modular, decoupled, and extensible news recommendation framework that includes a learner-friendly GUI, supports dataset processing, and allows training, validating, and testing models.

Result: NewsTorch ensures reproducible experiments, provides fair model comparisons using standardized metrics, and facilitates practical and conceptual learning for researchers.

Conclusion: NewsTorch enhances the learning experience and research efficiency in news recommendation systems, filling the gap in learner-targeted toolkits.

Abstract: News recommender systems are devised to alleviate the information overload, attracting more and more researchers' attention in recent years. The lack of a dedicated learner-oriented news recommendation toolkit hinders the advancement of research in news recommendation. We propose a PyTorch-based news recommendation toolkit called NewsTorch, developed to support learners in acquiring both conceptual understanding and practical experience. This toolkit provides a modular, decoupled, and extensible framework with a learner-friendly GUI platform that supports dataset downloading and preprocessing. It also enables training, validation, and testing of state-of-the-art neural news recommendation models with standardized evaluation metrics, ensuring fair comparison and reproducible experiments. Our open-source toolkit is released on Github: https://github.com/whonor/NewsTorch.

</details>


### [440] [CPGRec+: A Balance-oriented Framework for Personalized Video Game Recommendations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14586)
*Xiping Li,Aier Yang,Jianghong Ma,Kangzhe Liu,Shanshan Feng,Haijun Zhang,Yi Zhao*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces CPGRec+, an enhanced recommender system for gaming that uses edge reweighting and large language models (LLMs) to improve accuracy and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNN-based gaming recommender systems prioritize accuracy but often ignore diversity, and fail to address key disparities in player-game interactions. Additionally, these systems underutilize the reasoning abilities of LLMs.

Method: Two modules are introduced: (1) Preference-informed Edge Reweighting (PER), which assigns signed edge weights to differentiate significant player interests and prevents over-smoothing in GNNs. (2) Preference-informed Representation Generation (PRG), which uses LLMs to generate contextualized descriptions for players and games.

Result: Experiments on two Steam datasets confirm that CPGRec+ outperforms state-of-the-art models in both accuracy and diversity.

Conclusion: CPGRec+ effectively addresses the accuracy-diversity trade-off while leveraging LLMs to enhance contextual understanding and mitigate limitations of GNNs in gaming recommender systems.

Abstract: The rapid expansion of gaming industry requires advanced recommender systems tailored to its dynamic landscape. Existing Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based methods primarily prioritize accuracy over diversity, overlooking their inherent trade-off. To address this, we previously proposed CPGRec, a balance-oriented gaming recommender system. However, CPGRec fails to account for critical disparities in player-game interactions, which carry varying significance in reflecting players' personal preferences and may exacerbate over-smoothness issues inherent in GNN-based models. Moreover, existing approaches underutilize the reasoning capabilities and extensive knowledge of large language models (LLMs) in addressing these limitations. To bridge this gap, we propose two new modules. First, Preference-informed Edge Reweighting (PER) module assigns signed edge weights to qualitatively distinguish significant player interests and disinterests while then quantitatively measuring preference strength to mitigate over-smoothing in graph convolutions. Second, Preference-informed Representation Generation (PRG) module leverages LLMs to generate contextualized descriptions of games and players by reasoning personal preferences from comparing global and personal interests, thereby refining representations of players and games. Experiments on \textcolor{black}{two Steam datasets} demonstrate CPGRec+'s superior accuracy and diversity over state-of-the-art models. The code is accessible at https://github.com/HsipingLi/CPGRec-Plus.

</details>


### [441] [Uncertainty-aware Generative Learning Path Recommendation with Cognition-Adaptive Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14613)
*Xiangrui Xiong,Hang Liang,Baiyang Chen,Zifei Pan,Yanli Lee*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: U-GLAD introduces a novel framework for personalized learning path recommendations, addressing historical interaction uncertainty and diverse learning goals using probabilistic modeling and generative diffusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning path recommendation systems struggle with historical interaction uncertainty and lack adaptability to varied learner goals, leading to suboptimal personalization.

Method: The framework models cognitive states probabilistically with Gaussian LSTM to capture true learner states and uses a goal-oriented concept encoder with multi-head attention. A generative diffusion model predicts optimal concept recommendations.

Result: Extensive evaluations on three public datasets reveal U-GLAD outperforms existing methods, effectively addressing uncertainty and generating goal-aligned paths.

Conclusion: U-GLAD demonstrates superior adaptability and accuracy in personalized learning recommendations by leveraging probabilistic state modeling and generative techniques.

Abstract: Learning Path Recommendation (LPR) is critical for personalized education, yet current methods often fail to account for historical interaction uncertainty (e.g., lucky guesses or accidental slips) and lack adaptability to diverse learning goals. We propose U-GLAD (Uncertainty-aware Generative Learning Path Recommendation with Cognition-Adaptive Diffusion). To address representation bias, the framework models cognitive states as probability distributions, capturing the learner's underlying true state via a Gaussian LSTM. To ensure highly personalized recommendation, a goal-oriented concept encoder utilizes multi-head attention and objective-specific transformations to dynamically align concept semantics with individual learning goals, generating uniquely tailored embeddings. Unlike traditional discriminative ranking approaches, our model employs a generative diffusion model to predict the latent representation of the next optimal concept. Extensive evaluations on three public datasets demonstrate that U-GLAD significantly outperforms representative baselines. Further analyses confirm its superior capability in perceiving interaction uncertainty and providing stable, goal-driven recommendation paths.

</details>


### [442] [GenRec: A Preference-Oriented Generative Framework for Large-Scale Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14878)
*Yanyan Zou,Junbo Qi,Lunsong Huang,Yu Li,Kewei Xu,Jiabao Gao,Binglei Zhao,Xuanhua Yang,Sulong Xu,Shengjie Li*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: GenRec is a generative retrieval framework for recommendation addressing scalability challenges through innovative training objectives, input compression, and output alignment; achieving significant improvements in click and transaction counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations in scaling generative retrieval for industrial recommendation systems, particularly issues with output consistency, high input encoding costs, and alignment with nuanced user preferences.

Method: GenRec employs a single decoder-only architecture with three main innovations: Page-wise NTP for dense gradients and resolving training ambiguities, an asymmetric Token Merger for compressing semantic IDs without accuracy loss, and GRPO-SR reinforcement learning with hybrid rewards for stable and aligned outputs.

Result: Month-long online A/B tests on JD App showed GenRec improved click count by 9.5% and transaction count by 8.7% compared to the existing pipeline.

Conclusion: GenRec successfully scales generative retrieval for recommendation in industrial settings, providing a robust and user-preference-oriented framework with tangible performance benefits in real-world application.

Abstract: Generative Retrieval (GR) offers a promising paradigm for recommendation through next-token prediction (NTP). However, scaling it to large-scale industrial systems introduces three challenges: (i) within a single request, the identical model inputs may produce inconsistent outputs due to the pagination request mechanism; (ii) the prohibitive cost of encoding long user behavior sequences with multi-token item representations based on semantic IDs, and (iii) aligning the generative policy with nuanced user preference signals. We present GenRec, a preference-oriented generative framework deployed on the JD App that addresses above challenges within a single decoder-only architecture. For training objective, we propose Page-wise NTP task, which supervises over an entire interaction page rather than each interacted item individually, providing denser gradient signal and resolving the one-to-many ambiguity of point-wise training. On the prefilling side, an asymmetric linear Token Merger compresses multi-token Semantic IDs in the prompt while preserving full-resolution decoding, reducing input length by ~2X with negligible accuracy loss. To further align outputs with user satisfaction, we introduce GRPO-SR, a reinforcement learning method that pairs Group Relative Policy Optimization with NLL regularization for training stability, and employs Hybrid Rewards combining a dense reward model with a relevance gate to mitigate reward hacking. In month-long online A/B tests serving production traffic, GenRec achieves 9.5% improvement in click count and 8.7% in transaction count over the existing pipeline.

</details>


<div id='astro-ph.CO'></div>

# astro-ph.CO [[Back]](#toc)

### [443] [FAIR Universe Weak Lensing ML Uncertainty Challenge: Handling Uncertainties and Distribution Shifts for Precision Cosmology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14451)
*Biwei Dai,Po-Wen Chang,Wahid Bhimji,Paolo Calafiura,Ragansu Chakkappai,Yuan-Tang Chou,Sascha Diefenbacher,Jordan Dudley,Ibrahim Elsharkawy,Steven Farrell,Isabelle Guyon,Chris Harris,Elham E Khoda,Benjamin Nachman,David Rousseau,Uroš Seljak,Ihsan Ullah,Yulei Zhang*

Main category: astro-ph.CO

TL;DR: The abstract introduces a standardized benchmark dataset and challenge for using machine learning in weak gravitational lensing to address issues like simulation costs and inaccuracies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weak gravitational lensing provides insights into the matter distribution and cosmological models, but existing methods face limitations due to simulation-based training and systematics.

Method: They propose a weak lensing benchmark dataset with realistic systematics and launch a structured challenge to improve ML methodologies under constrained conditions.

Result: The challenge encourages collaboration between physics and ML communities to improve data handling and analysis methods for weak lensing.

Conclusion: The effort aims to standardize benchmarks, address uncertainties, and enable ML methods in future weak lensing surveys.

Abstract: Weak gravitational lensing, the correlated distortion of background galaxy shapes by foreground structures, is a powerful probe of the matter distribution in our universe and allows accurate constraints on the cosmological model. In recent years, high-order statistics and machine learning (ML) techniques have been applied to weak lensing data to extract the nonlinear information beyond traditional two-point analysis. However, these methods typically rely on cosmological simulations, which poses several challenges: simulations are computationally expensive, limiting most realistic setups to a low training data regime; inaccurate modeling of systematics in the simulations create distribution shifts that can bias cosmological parameter constraints; and varying simulation setups across studies make method comparison difficult. To address these difficulties, we present the first weak lensing benchmark dataset with several realistic systematics and launch the FAIR Universe Weak Lensing Machine Learning Uncertainty Challenge. The challenge focuses on measuring the fundamental properties of the universe from weak lensing data with limited training set and potential distribution shifts, while providing a standardized benchmark for rigorous comparison across methods. Organized in two phases, the challenge will bring together the physics and ML communities to advance the methodologies for handling systematic uncertainties, data efficiency, and distribution shifts in weak lensing analysis with ML, ultimately facilitating the deployment of ML approaches into upcoming weak lensing survey analysis.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [444] [Cellwise Outliers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14182)
*Mia Hubert,Jakob Raymaekers,Peter J. Rousseeuw*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper reviews advancements in cellwise outlier detection and robust methods for high-dimensional datasets, proposing alternatives to traditional casewise approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenges posed by cellwise outliers in modern datasets, highlighting their potential to contaminate analyses and the need for novel approaches.

Method: A review of existing methodologies for detecting cellwise outliers and constructing robust techniques across diverse statistical applications, including location estimation, covariance matrices, regression, PCA, and tensor data.

Result: Cellwise approaches are shown to effectively handle high-dimensional data and missing values, marking improvements over traditional casewise methods.

Conclusion: The cellwise approach is crucial for modern statistics and machine learning, offering robust solutions for challenging high-dimensional and incomplete datasets.

Abstract: In statistics and machine learning, the traditional meaning of the terms `outlier' and `anomaly' is a case in the dataset that behaves differently from the bulk of the data. This raises suspicion that it may belong to a different population. But nowadays increasing attention is being paid to so-called cellwise outliers. These are individual values somewhere in the data matrix (or data tensor). Depending on the dimension, even a relatively small proportion of outlying cells can contaminate over half the cases, which is a problem for existing casewise methods. It turns out that detecting cellwise outliers as well as constructing cellwise robust methods requires techniques that are quite different from the casewise setting. For instance, one has to let go of some intuitive equivariance properties. The problem is difficult, but the past decade has seen substantial progress. For high-dimensional data the cellwise approach is becoming dominant, and typically can deal with missing values as well. We review developments in the estimation of location and covariance matrices as well as regression methods, principal component analysis, methods for tensor data, and various other settings.

</details>


### [445] [Combining Bayesian and Frequentist Inference for Laboratory-Specific Performance Guarantees in Copy Number Variation Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14305)
*Austin Talbot,Alex V. Kotlar,Yue Ke*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenges of providing reliable per-gene performance guarantees for copy number variant (CNV) detection in oncology diagnostics using targeted amplicon panels, proposing a hybrid framework that resolves miscalibration issues via frequentist tolerance intervals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Targeted amplicon panels are crucial in oncology diagnostics, but they face difficulties in ensuring reliability for CNV detection due to factors like amplification artifacts and limited sample sizes.

Method: The paper introduces a hybrid framework that combines Bayesian posterior functionals with Gamma-distributed models for squared losses, enabling frequentist tolerance intervals with valid coverage rates. Key elements include imputation, regularization, and evidence-based stratification.

Result: Empirical evaluations show significantly improved coverage accuracy across genes in both process-matched and unmatched conditions, outperforming Bayesian benchmarks that suffer severe miscalibration.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid method effectively addresses the limitations of Bayesian CNV callers and miscalibration issues, offering a practical solution for clinical validation of targeted amplicon panels.

Abstract: Targeted amplicon panels are widely used in oncology diagnostics, but providing per-gene performance guarantees for copy number variant (CNV) detection remains challenging due to amplification artifacts, process-mismatch heterogeneity, and limited validation sample sizes. While Bayesian CNV callers naturally quantify per-sample uncertainty, translating this into the frequentist population-level guarantees required for clinical validation, coverage rates, false-positive bounds, and minimum detectable copy-number changes, is a fundamentally different inferential problem. We show empirically that even robust Bayesian credible intervals, including coarsened posteriors and sandwich-adjusted intervals, are severely miscalibrated on panels with small amplicon counts per gene. To address this, we propose a hybrid framework that evaluates Bayesian posterior functionals on validation samples and models the resulting squared losses with a Gamma distribution, yielding tolerance intervals with valid frequentist coverage. Three components make the method practical under real-world constraints: (1) imputation that removes the influence of true CNV-positive samples without requiring known ground truth, (2) regularization to address small sample variability, and (3) evidence-based stratification on the log model evidence to accommodate non-exchangeable noise profiles arising from process mismatch. Evaluated on two targeted amplicon panels using leave-one-out cross-validation, the proposed method achieves single-digit mean absolute coverage error across all genes under both process-matched and unmatched conditions, whereas Bayesian comparators exhibit mean absolute errors exceeding 60\% on clinically relevant genes such as ERBB2.

</details>


### [446] [PROXIMA: A Reliability Scoring Framework for Proxy Metrics in Online Controlled Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14352)
*Avinash Amudala*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper introduces PROXIMA, a diagnostic framework assessing the reliability of proxy metrics in A/B testing, validated on two datasets, achieving high decision accuracy and uncovering segment-level heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the problem of unreliable proxy metrics in large-scale online A/B testing, where aggregate measures can lead to incorrect decisions due to heterogeneous relationships across user segments.

Method: PROXIMA is proposed to evaluate proxy reliability using three dimensions: normalised effect correlation, directional accuracy, and segment-level fragility rate. The framework audits proxies for correct launch decisions and highlights failures in user segments.

Result: PROXIMA achieves 98.4% decision agreement with an oracle policy across the Criteo and KuaiRec datasets, showing strong composite reliability scores and revealing significant heterogeneity in recommendation domains compared to advertising.

Conclusion: PROXIMA effectively discriminates reliable from unreliable proxies, demonstrating robustness in auditing proxy metrics and highlighting segment-level fragility for more informed decision-making.

Abstract: Online A/B testing at scale relies on proxy metrics -- short-term, easily-measured signals used in place of slow-moving long-term outcomes. When the proxy-outcome relationship is heterogeneous across user segments, aggregate correlation can mask directional failures akin to Simpson's Paradox, leading to costly ship/no-ship errors. We introduce PROXIMA (Proxy Metric Validation Framework for Online Experiments), a lightweight diagnostic framework that scores proxy reliability through a composite of three complementary dimensions: normalised effect correlation, directional accuracy, and segment-level fragility rate. Unlike surrogate-index approaches that predict long-term treatment effects, PROXIMA directly audits whether a candidate proxy leads to correct launch decisions and flags the user segments where it fails. We validate PROXIMA on two public datasets -- the Criteo Uplift corpus (14M observations, advertising) and KuaiRec (7K users, video recommendation) -- using 80 simulated A/B tests. Early engagement metrics achieve a composite reliability of 0.80 on Criteo and 0.62 on KuaiRec, yielding 98.4% average decision agreement with an oracle policy. Fragility analysis reveals that recommendation domains exhibit substantially higher segment-level heterogeneity (68% fragility) than advertising (13%), yet directional accuracy remains above 96% in both cases. A sensitivity analysis over the weight space confirms that no single component suffices and that the composite provides substantially better discrimination between reliable and unreliable proxies than correlation alone. Code and reproduction scripts are available at: https://github.com/Avinash-Amudala/PROXIMA

</details>


### [447] [Deployment of AI-Assisted Interventions: Capacity Constraints and Noisy Compliance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14370)
*Carri W. Chan,Yi Han,Hannah Li,Benjamin L. Ranard*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: AI tools guiding targeted interventions often prioritize predictive accuracy, but this approach is suboptimal when service capacity is limited. This study introduces an optimal thresholding method and a new metric, OpAUC, for better operational performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the inefficiencies in algorithm selection and threshold setting for interventions, focusing on cases where service capacity limitations and probabilistic responses play significant roles.

Method: Authors characterize the balance between utilization and cannibalization in service allocation, propose optimal thresholding, introduce Operational AUC (OpAUC) for algorithm selection, and validate their approach with a sepsis early warning case study.

Result: The study demonstrates that predictive accuracy as a sole criterion can lead to suboptimal outcomes, and using OpAUC results in more aligned algorithm selection with operational goals.

Conclusion: Policies based solely on predictive accuracy are generally misaligned with operational performance; OpAUC offers a superior method for algorithm and threshold optimization, improving intervention outcomes under limited capacities.

Abstract: AI tools increasingly guide targeted interventions in healthcare, education, and recruiting. Algorithms score individuals, trigger outreach to those above a threshold (e.g., high-risk or high-value), and encourage them to request service; then providers deliver service to those who request. Standard practice sets the threshold and selects the algorithm to maximize predictive accuracy, assuming that better predictions yield better outcomes. We show that this approach is suboptimal when limited service capacity and probabilistic behavioral responses influence who receives service. In such settings, the optimal score threshold must balance two effects: ensuring all capacity is filled (utilization) and ensuring high-value individuals are served despite competition between requests (cannibalization). We characterize the optimal threshold and prove that policies based solely on predictive accuracy are generally suboptimal. Further, because optimal thresholds vary with service capacity, algorithm selection metrics like AUC, which weight all thresholds equally, are misaligned with operational performance. We introduce a new metric--Operational AUC (OpAUC)--and show it leads to optimal algorithm selection. Finally, we conduct a case study on sepsis early warning data and illustrate the magnitude of improvement that can be achieved from improved threshold and algorithm selection.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [448] [Mamba-SSM with LLM Reasoning for Biomarker Discovery: Causal Feature Refinement via Chain-of-Thought Gene Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14334)
*Pushpa Kumar Balan,Aijing Feng*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper applies LLM chain-of-thought reasoning to filter tissue-composition confounders from gradient saliency-selected gene sets, improving downstream classification performance despite some loss of biomarker faithfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biomarker discovery using gradient saliency is hindered by confounding factors affecting classifier performance. The study aims to evaluate if LLM chain-of-thought reasoning can target and filter these confounders effectively.

Method: The paper utilizes a trained Mamba SSM on RNA-seq data to extract a preliminary top-50 gene set via gradient saliency. DeepSeek-R1 then employs LLM chain-of-thought reasoning to filter these genes, resulting in a final set of 17 genes.

Result: The LLM-filtered gene set improves classification performance over both the raw saliency set and baseline gene sets, achieving AUC 0.927 while using significantly fewer features. However, only 35.3% of the filtered genes are validated biomarkers, with some known BRCA genes excluded.

Conclusion: Targeted reasoning (selective faithfulness) from LLMs effectively removes confounders and enhances performance despite incomplete biomarker recall, indicating that perfect reasoning faithfulness may not be necessary for downstream success.

Abstract: Gradient saliency from deep sequence models surfaces candidate biomarkers efficiently, but the resulting gene lists are contaminated by tissue-composition confounders that degrade downstream classifiers. We study whether LLM chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning can faithfully filter these confounders, and whether reasoning quality drives downstream performance. We train a Mamba SSM on TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq and extract the top-50 genes by gradient saliency; DeepSeek-R1 evaluates every candidate with structured CoT to produce a final 17-gene set. The raw 50-gene saliency set (no LLM) performs worse than a 5,000-gene variance baseline (AUC 0.832 vs. 0.903), while the LLM-filtered set surpasses it (AUC 0.927), using 294x fewer features. A faithfulness audit (COSMIC CGC, OncoKB, PAM50) reveals only 6 of 17 selected genes (35.3%) are validated BRCA biomarkers, yet 10 of 16 known BRCA genes in the input were missed - including FOXA1. This gap between downstream performance and reasoning faithfulness suggests selective faithfulness: targeted confounder removal is sufficient for performance gains even without comprehensive recall.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [449] [Separation is Optimal for LQR under Intermittent Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.27833)
*Abdullah Y. Etcibasi,C. Emre Koksal,Eylem Ekici*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper addresses communication-constrained LQR problems and proves the separation principle while establishing optimal scheduling and controller policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to solve the optimization of LQR problems under communication constraints, particularly focusing on disturbances.

Method: The authors use dynamic programming to prove the separation principle and derive optimal scheduling and control policies.

Result: The optimal scheduling policy is a symmetric threshold rule, and the optimal controller is a discounted linear feedback law independent of scheduling.

Conclusion: This research confirms the separation principle and provides optimal solutions for scheduling and control in communication-constrained LQR problems.

Abstract: In this work, we first prove that the separation principle holds for communication-constrained LQR problems under i.i.d. zero-mean disturbances with a symmetric distribution. We then solve the dynamic programming problem and show that the optimal scheduling policy is a symmetric threshold rule on the accumulated disturbance since the most recent update, while the optimal controller is a discounted linear feedback law independent of the scheduling policy.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [450] [Distributed Variational Quantum Linear Solver](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14435)
*Chao Lu,Pooja Rao,Muralikrishnan Gopalakrishnan Meena,Kalyana Chakaravarthi Gottiparthi*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper addresses the scalability bottleneck of the Variational Quantum Linear Solver (VQLS) by proposing distributed computation (D-VQLS) and a fast Walsh--Hadamard transform-based compression method to significantly reduce circuit evaluation and computational costs, achieving high fidelity and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the scalability challenges posed by the Linear Combination of Unitaries (LCU) decomposition in VQLS, which becomes computationally prohibitive as the system size increases.

Method: The authors introduce two strategies: (1) Distributed VQLS (D-VQLS) to distribute cost-function evaluations across hardware through NVIDIA CUDA-Q, and (2) fast Walsh--Hadamard transform-based Pauli decomposition for reducing circuit complexity and the number of terms involved in the computation.

Result: The proposed methods achieve a significant reduction in circuit evaluations (from 23 million to 90,112 for a 10-qubit case) while maintaining high solution fidelity (>99.99%). Additionally, distributed simulations using up to 96 GPUs demonstrate high strong and weak scaling efficiencies.

Conclusion: The study enhances the scalability and practicality of VQLS by reducing computational expenses and leveraging distributed computing, making it more feasible for early fault-tolerant quantum processing units (QPUs).

Abstract: The Variational Quantum Linear Solver (VQLS), a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm for solving linear systems, faces a practical scalability bottleneck: the Linear Combination of Unitaries (LCU) decomposition requires O(L^2) circuit evaluations per optimizer iteration, where $L$ can grow as 4^n for n-qubit systems for the worst case scenario. We address this computational bottleneck through two complementary strategies. First, we present a distributed VQLS (D-VQLS) framework, built on NVIDIA CUDA-Q, that enables asynchronous, scalable distribution of the O(L^2) cost-function evaluations. Second, a fast Walsh--Hadamard transform (FWHT)-based Pauli decomposition with 1% coefficient thresholding curbs the exponential growth of LCU terms, reducing L from O}(2^n) to O(1) for n > 6 qubits and compressing the per-iteration circuit complexity from O(n * 4^n) to O(n) for sparse, structured matrices. For a 10-qubit tridiagonal Toeplitz system, this yields a 256x reduction, from 23 million to 90,112 circuits per iteration, while preserving over $99.99\%$ solution fidelity. Additionally, to inform feasibility on early fault-tolerant QPUs, the paper provides resource estimates -- gate counts, qubit requirements, and circuit evaluations per iteration -- for VQLS applied to arbitrary matrices. The D-VQLS framework is validated on the NERSC Perlmutter supercomputer using multi-node, multi-GPU ideal state-vector simulations, achieving over 99.99% fidelity against classical solutions on tridiagonal Toeplitz and Hele--Shaw flow benchmarks, with near-ideal strong scaling up to 24 GPUs and 95.3% weak scaling efficiency at 96 GPUs processing 360,448 circuits per iteration for a 10-qubit system. Systematic profiling identifies the optimal resource allocation for distributed quantum circuit workloads, yielding a 2.52x speedup for the configurations studied.

</details>


### [451] [Magnitude Is All You Need? Rethinking Phase in Quantum Encoding of Complex SAR Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14229)
*Sakthi Prabhu Gunasekar,Prasanna Kumar R*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The study evaluates quantum encoding strategies for Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR). It finds that magnitude-only encoding performs better in hybrid quantum-classical architectures, while phase information is crucial in purely quantum models.


<details>
